**Anhui**[[a]](#cite_note-7) is an inland [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China") located in [East China](/wiki/East_China "East China"). Its provincial capital and largest city is [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei"). The province is located across the basins of the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") and [Huai rivers](/wiki/Huai_river "Huai river"), bordering [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") and [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") to the east, [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi") to the south, [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") and [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") to the west, and [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong") to the north. With a population of 61 million, Anhui is the 9th most populous province in China. It is the 22nd largest Chinese province based on area, and the 12th most densely populated region of all 34 Chinese provincial regions. Anhui's population is mostly composed of [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese"). Languages spoken within the province include [Lower Yangtze Mandarin](/wiki/Lower_Yangtze_Mandarin "Lower Yangtze Mandarin"), [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese"), [Hui](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese"), [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese") and small portion of [Central Plains Mandarin](/wiki/Central_Plains_Mandarin "Central Plains Mandarin").

The name "Anhui" derives from the names of two cities: [Anqing](/wiki/Anqing "Anqing") and [Huizhou](/wiki/Huizhou,_Anhui "Huizhou, Anhui") (now [Huangshan City](/wiki/Huangshan_City "Huangshan City")).[[7]](#cite_note-8) The abbreviation for Anhui is *Wǎn* (皖), corresponding to the historical [Wan state](/w/index.php?title=State_of_Wan&action=edit&redlink=1 "State of Wan (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9A%96%E5%9C%8B "zh:皖國")], and is also used to refer to the [Wan River](/wiki/Wan_River "Wan River") and [Mount Tianzhu](/wiki/Mount_Tianzhu "Mount Tianzhu"). The provincial government of Anhui includes a Governor, Provincial Congress, the People's Political Consultative Conference, and the Provincial Higher People's Court. Aside from managing local government departments, the Anhui provincial government manages 16 cities, 62 counties, 43 county-level districts and 1,522 townships. Anhui's total GDP ranked 14th among China's 31 province-level regions as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/%E4%B8%89%E6%B2%B3%E5%8F%A4%E9%95%87%E6%9C%9D%E9%98%B3%E6%A5%BC_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%89%E6%B2%B3%E5%8F%A4%E9%95%87%E6%9C%9D%E9%98%B3%E6%A5%BC_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%89%E6%B2%B3%E5%8F%A4%E9%95%87%E6%9C%9D%E9%98%B3%E6%A5%BC_-_panoramio.jpg)

The old town of [Sanhe, Feixi County](/wiki/Sanhe,_Feixi_County "Sanhe, Feixi County")

Anhui was established 1667, during the reign of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty")'s [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor"). The province's nickname, "Wan", is taken from the name of a small state that existed in the region during the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (722–481 BC). The name "Wan" also corresponds to the province's [Mount Tianzhu](/wiki/Mount_Tianzhu "Mount Tianzhu") and [Wan state](/w/index.php?title=State_of_Wan&action=edit&redlink=1 "State of Wan (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9A%96%E5%9C%8B "zh:皖國")]. Evidence of human beings inhabiting what is now Anhui 20,000 years before present has been attested by archaeological findings in [Fanchang County](/wiki/Fanchang_County "Fanchang County"), related to the cultural domains of [Yangshao](/wiki/Yangshao_culture "Yangshao culture") and [Longshan](/wiki/Longshan_culture "Longshan culture"), dated to the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period (8000–2000 BC). In relation to these cultures, archeologists have discovered through excavation a 4500-year-old city called the Nanchengzi Ruins in [Guzhen County](/wiki/Guzhen_County "Guzhen County"), after they discovered a Neolithic city wall and a moat that was part of a much larger and integrated city in the region during their 2013 disinterment.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Anhui_quan_tu_LOC_96685910.jpg/250px-Anhui_quan_tu_LOC_96685910.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anhui_quan_tu_LOC_96685910.jpg)

There are many historical sites located in the province dating to the pre-imperial period. The culture of northern Anhui was associated with that of the [North China Plain](/wiki/North_China_Plain "North China Plain"), together with what is now Henan, northern Jiangsu, and southern Shandong. Central Anhui was densely populated, and consisted of the fertile [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") watershed. Contrastingly, southern Anhui was closer to [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi") and southern [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"); the hills in the southeastern part of the province formed a unique cultural sphere. After the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") unified China in 221 BC, the territory of modern Anhui belonged to different prefectures, including Jiujiang, Zhang, Tang, and Sishui. The lands became part of the [Yang](/wiki/Yang_prefecture "Yang prefecture"), [Yu](/wiki/Y%C3%B9_Prefecture "Yù Prefecture"), and Xu prefectures during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BC – 220 AD). During the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") era (222–280), Anhui was dominated at various times by [Eastern Wu](/wiki/Eastern_Wu "Eastern Wu") and [Cao Wei](/wiki/Cao_Wei "Cao Wei"). By the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") (960–1279), the economy and culture of area was flourishing. Anhui became part of the province of [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") during the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") (1271–1368); during the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") the area was directly administrated by the imperial capital in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing").

Anhui and [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") were merged into one province under the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") (1644–1912) until 1666, during the reign of the [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor"). It played an important role in the [Self-Strengthening Movement](/wiki/Self-Strengthening_Movement "Self-Strengthening Movement") led by Li Hongzhang during the later Qing. During this time, western weapons, factories and administrative theories were being introduced into China. Over the next 50 years, Anhui became one of the country's most radically liberal areas, with important 20th-century figures moving to the province, like the poet and diplomat [Hu Shih](/wiki/Hu_Shih "Hu Shih"), and [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu"), the founder of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP).

In 1938, large areas in north and central Anhui were severely damaged by the decision of President [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") to demolish a critical dam on the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River"), hoping that would physically impede the ongoing invasion by the [Imperial Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") and prevent them from capturing [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"). Within only ten days of the dam breaking, the water and sands drowned all of north and middle area of this province, resulting in the deaths of between 500,000 and 900,000 Chinese people, as well as an unknown number of Japanese soldiers. Following the end of the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") in 1945, the capital city of Anhui moved to [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei"), then a small town. The provincial government made significant investments for its development. After 1949, the government launched projects to repair the damages suffered during World War II. Anhui was one of the worst affected provinces in [The Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/The_Great_Chinese_Famine "The Great Chinese Famine"). The province underwent significant development via the [Third Front](/wiki/Third_Front_(China) "Third Front (China)") campaign to build basic and national defense industries in protected locations, in case of invasion by the Soviet Union or United States. The centerpiece of the Shanghai Small Third Front was the "rear base" in Anhui which served as "a multi-function manufacturing base for anti-aircraft and anti-tank weaponry".[[9]](#cite_note-:2-10) During the late 1990s Anhui became one of China's fastest-growing provinces. The province forms part of the China Yangtze River Delta Economic Area, which is the most developed area of China.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Anhui is topographically diverse. The north is part of the [North China Plain](/wiki/North_China_Plain "North China Plain") while the north-central areas are part of the [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") watershed. Both regions are flat, and densely populated. The land becomes more uneven further south, with the [Dabie Mountains](/wiki/Dabie_Mountains "Dabie Mountains") occupying much of southwestern Anhui and a series of hills and ranges cutting through southeastern Anhui, between which is the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"). The highest peak in Anhui is Lotus Peak, part of [Huangshan](/wiki/Huangshan "Huangshan") in southeastern Anhui. It has an altitude of 1,873 metres (6,145 feet).

Major rivers include the [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") in the north and the Yangtze in the south. The largest lake is [Lake Chaohu](/wiki/Lake_Chaohu "Lake Chaohu") situated in the center of the province, with an area of about 800 km2 (310 sq mi). The southeastern part of the province near the Yangtze River has many lakes as well.

As with topography, the province differs in climate from north to south. The north is more temperate with more distinct seasons. January temperatures average at around −1 to 2 °C north of the Huai River, and 0 to 3 °C south of the Huai River; in July temperatures average 27 °C or above. [Plum rains](/wiki/East_Asian_rainy_season "East Asian rainy season") occur in June and July and may cause flooding.

Anhui has 16 cities. In economic terms, the top three cities are: [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei"), [Wuhu](/wiki/Wuhu "Wuhu") and [Anqing](/wiki/Anqing "Anqing").

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ecology")]

The Anhui elm*, Ulmus* *gaussenii* W. C. Cheng, is a medium size [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") tree whose natural range is restricted to the valleys of the [Langya](/wiki/Mount_Langya_(Anhui) "Mount Langya (Anhui)") limestone mountains of [Chu Xian](/w/index.php?title=Chu_Xian&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chu Xian (page does not exist)").[[10]](#cite_note-Geographical-11) The tree was most commonly found on the flood plains, indicating a tolerance of periodic inundation. *U. gaussenii* is now the world's rarest and most endangered elm species, with only approximately 30 trees known to survive in the wild in 2009.[[11]](#cite_note-Grimshaw-12)

The province is also home to the [Chinese alligator](/wiki/Chinese_alligator "Chinese alligator") (*Alligator sinensis*, [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 扬子鳄; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): [揚子](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%8F%9A%E5%AD%90 "wikt:揚子")[鱷](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%B1%B7 "wikt:鱷"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *yángzǐ'è*), also known as the Yangtze alligator,[[12]](#cite_note-undp-13) China alligator,[[13]](#cite_note-iucn-14) or historically the muddy dragon,[[14]](#cite_note-latimes-15) a [critically endangered](/wiki/Critically_endangered "Critically endangered") [crocodilian](/wiki/Crocodilian "Crocodilian").

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of Anhui](/wiki/Politics_of_Anhui "Politics of Anhui") and [List of provincial leaders of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/List_of_provincial_leaders_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "List of provincial leaders of the People's Republic of China")

The Politics of Anhui Province is structured in a dual party-government system like all other governing institutions in mainland China. The [Governor of Anhui](/wiki/Governor_of_Anhui "Governor of Anhui") is the highest-ranking official in the Anhui Provincial People's Government. However, in the province's dual party-government governing system, the Governor has less power than the [Anhui Chinese Communist Party Provincial Committee Secretary](/wiki/Party_Secretary_of_Anhui "Party Secretary of Anhui").

Most provincial government departments and the Governor office are located at No.1 Zhongshan Road, moved from old downtown of Hefei since 2016. Provincial government is responsible to manage 16 prefecture-level cities, 105 counties, 1845 townships and different departments in Anhui. The Anhui Provincial People's Congress is just located near provincial government. The Provincial [Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Political_Consultative_Conference "Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference") (CPPCC) is located at No.317, Suzhou Road. The Provincial Higher People's Court is located at no. 472 Changjiang Rd.

Typically, annual provincial congress meeting is held in the first quarter of each year shortly before national congress meeting and the annual meeting of CPPCC is held in the first quarter or the fourth quarter of each year.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economy")]

Agriculture in Anhui varies according to the climate zones. To the north of the [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River"), [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") and [sweet potatoes](/wiki/Sweet_potato "Sweet potato") are grown, while to the south it is [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") and wheat instead. As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit), Anhui's [nominal GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") was [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")670 billion ([CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") 4.51 trilion), about 3.72% of the country's GDP and ranked [10th](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_provincial-level_divisions_by_GDP "List of Chinese provincial-level divisions by GDP") among [province-level administrative units](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_China "Administrative divisions of China"); the province's primary, secondary and tertiary industries were worth CN¥351.37 billion (US$52.24 billion), CN¥1.8588 trillion (US$276.36 billion) and CN¥2.29433 trillion (US$341.1 billion) respectively. Its nominal GDP per capita was US$10,943 (CN¥73,603) and ranked the [14th](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_provincial-level_divisions_by_GDP_per_capita "List of Chinese provincial-level divisions by GDP per capita") in the country.[[3]](#cite_note-GDPdata-3)

Natural resources of Anhui include [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") in [Ma'anshan](/wiki/Ma%27anshan "Ma'anshan"), [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") in [Huainan](/wiki/Huainan "Huainan"), and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") in [Tongling](/wiki/Tongling "Tongling"). There are industries related to these natural resources (e.g. [steel industry](/wiki/Steel_industry "Steel industry") at Ma'anshan). One of the famous Anhui-based corporations is the automobile company [Chery](/wiki/Chery "Chery"), which is based in [Wuhu](/wiki/Wuhu_City "Wuhu City").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Chuzhao_Dingyuan_Lianpuxiang_-_Nongke_Hanlin_Zhu_IMG_9222.jpg/250px-Chuzhao_Dingyuan_Lianpuxiang_-_Nongke_Hanlin_Zhu_IMG_9222.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chuzhao_Dingyuan_Lianpuxiang_-_Nongke_Hanlin_Zhu_IMG_9222.jpg)

Farmlands of Anhui

Compared to its more prosperous neighbours to the east, [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") and [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), Anhui has lagged markedly behind in economic development, with a [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") per capita around half of those two provinces in 2017 rapidly improved from 1/3 of those two provinces in 2010. However, the provincial GDP per capita is based on the population registered in the province (that is, with local [Hukou](/wiki/Hukou "Hukou")), but not necessarily residing there. There is significant regional disparity, where much of the wealth is concentrated in industrial regions close to the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), such as [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei"), Wuhu, and Ma'anshan. The province is home to a large cluster of white goods manufacture such as [Haier](/wiki/Haier "Haier"), Hisense, Whirlpool, Gree, Royalstar, and Meling.

### Major economic and technological development zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Major economic and technological development zones")]

#### Hefei Economic and Technological Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hefei Economic and Technological Development Zone")]

Hefei Economic and Technological Development Zone is located in the southwest of Hefei and was established in 1993. It is located close to Hefei Luogang International Airport.[[21]](#cite_note-28)

#### Hefei Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hefei Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone")]

Hefei Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone was founded in October 1990 and approved by the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_China "State Council of China") as a state-level Development Zone in March 1991. In 1997, the Development Zone was ratified as an APEC Science and Technology Industrial Park, with special open policies to APEC and EU members. Hefei High Tech Park was also approved as a National High Tech Export Base in 2000 and obtained the award of an Advanced High Tech Zone under the Torch Program in 2003. So far, more than 100 hi-tech enterprises have entered the zone. Industries encouraged in the zone include chemical production and processing, electronics assembly & manufacturing, instruments & industrial equipment, medical Equipment and telecommunications.[[22]](#cite_note-29)

#### Wuhu Economic and Technological Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Wuhu Economic and Technological Development Zone")]

Established in 1993, Wuhu Economic and Technological Development Zone was the first state-level development zone approved by central government in Anhui, utilising the transportation advantage of the Yangtse Delta at [Wuhu](/wiki/Wuhu "Wuhu").[[23]](#cite_note-30)

#### Wuhu Export Processing Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Wuhu Export Processing Zone")]

Wuhu Export Processing Zone was approved to be a national level export processing zone, with a total planned area of 2.95 km2 (1.14 sq mi).[[24]](#cite_note-31)

a metro entrance in Hefei

Historically, Anhui's transport network was hampered by the lack of bridges across the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), which divides the province into northern and southern regions. The first bridge across the Yangtze in Anhui, the [Tongling Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Tongling_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Tongling Yangtze River Bridge"), was completed in 1995. As of October 2014, Anhui had [four bridges](/wiki/Yangtze_River_bridges_and_tunnels "Yangtze River bridges and tunnels") across the Yangtze, at [Ma'anshan](/wiki/Ma%27anshan_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Ma'anshan Yangtze River Bridge"), [Wuhu](/wiki/Wuhu_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Wuhu Yangtze River Bridge"), Tongling, and [Anqing](/wiki/Anqing_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Anqing Yangtze River Bridge").

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rail")]

Anhui lacked a developed railway network until this century: most cities are now connected by a high-speed train system. [Hefei South railway station](/wiki/Hefei_South_railway_station "Hefei South railway station") is the high-speed train hub.

### Highway system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Highway system")]

The province set an ambitious plan from 2015 to 2025 for highways including:

[G3 Beijing-Taipei Expressway](/wiki/G3_Beijing-Taipei_Expressway "G3 Beijing-Taipei Expressway")

[G40 Shanghai-Xi'an Expressway](/wiki/G40_Shanghai-Xi%27an_Expressway "G40 Shanghai-Xi'an Expressway")

[G42 Shanghai-Chengdu Expressway](/wiki/G42_Shanghai-Chengdu_Expressway "G42 Shanghai-Chengdu Expressway")

S24 Changshu-Hefei Expressway

### Subway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Subway")]

Hefei and Wuhu have subway systems. The [Hefei Metro](/wiki/Hefei_Metro "Hefei Metro") has 2 completed lines, 3 lines under construction and another 10 lines planned. The [Wuhu Metro](/wiki/Wuhu_Metro "Wuhu Metro") has 2 subway lines under construction and another 3 lines planned.

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Aviation")]

The province has 5 major commercial airports and another 4 are under construction. Hefei Xinqiao International Airport and Huangshan International Airport are the 2 international airports. The 5 airports in operation are:

* [Hefei Xinqiao International Airport](/wiki/Hefei_Xinqiao_International_Airport "Hefei Xinqiao International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **HFE**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **ZSOF**)
* [Huangshan Tunxi International Airport](/wiki/Huangshan_Tunxi_International_Airport "Huangshan Tunxi International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **TXN**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **ZSTX**)
* [Fuyang Xiguan Airport](/wiki/Fuyang_Xiguan_Airport "Fuyang Xiguan Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **FUG**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **ZSFY**)
* [Anqing Tianzhushan Airport](/wiki/Anqing_Tianzhushan_Airport "Anqing Tianzhushan Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **AQG**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **ZSAQ**)
* [Chizhou Jiuhuashan Airport](/wiki/Chizhou_Jiuhuashan_Airport "Chizhou Jiuhuashan Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **JUH**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **ZSJH**)

Anhui has a highly unbalanced gender ratio. According to a 2009 study published in the *[British Medical Journal](/wiki/British_Medical_Journal "British Medical Journal")*, in the 1–4 age group, there are 138 boys for every 100 girls, making it among the most unbalanced of provinces in China.[[35]](#cite_note-42)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religion in Anhui[[36]](#cite_note-Wang2015-44)[[note 2]](#cite_note-46)

1. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (5.30%)
2. [Chinese ancestral religion](/wiki/Chinese_ancestral_religion "Chinese ancestral religion") (4.64%)
3. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (0.58%)
4. Other religions or not religious people[[note 1]](#cite_note-43) (89.5%)

The predominant religions in Anhui are [Chinese folk religions](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), [Taoist traditions](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") and [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"). According to surveys conducted in 2007 and 2009, 4.64% of the population believes and is involved in [ancestor veneration](/wiki/Chinese_ancestral_religion "Chinese ancestral religion"), while 5.30% of the population identifies as Christian.[[36]](#cite_note-Wang2015-44) According to a 2010 survey, Muslims constitute 0.58% of the population of Anhui.[[37]](#cite_note-2010-Islam-45)

The reports didn't give figures for other types of religion; 89.48% of the population may be either irreligious or involved in [worship of nature deities](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), Buddhism, [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), Taoism and [folk religious sects](/wiki/Chinese_salvationist_religions "Chinese salvationist religions").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anhui&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Culture")]

Anhui spans many geographical and cultural regions. The northern, flatter parts of the province, along the [Huai River](/wiki/Huai_River "Huai River") and further north, are most akin to neighboring provinces like [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong") and northern [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"). In contrast, the southern, hilly parts of the province are more similar in culture and dialect to other southern, hilly provinces, like [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") and [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi").

[Mandarin dialects](/wiki/Mandarin_dialects "Mandarin dialects") are spoken over the northern and central parts of the province, north of the Yangtze river. Dialects to the north (e.g. [Bengbu dialect](/w/index.php?title=Bengbu_dialect&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bengbu dialect (page does not exist)")) are classified as [Zhongyuan Mandarin](/wiki/Zhongyuan_Mandarin "Zhongyuan Mandarin"), together with dialects in provinces such as Henan and Shandong; dialects in the central parts (e.g. [Hefei dialect](/wiki/Hefei_dialect "Hefei dialect")) are classified as [Jianghuai Mandarin](/wiki/Jianghuai_Mandarin "Jianghuai Mandarin"), together with dialects in the central parts of neighboring [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") province. Non-Mandarin dialects are spoken to the south of the Yangzi: dialects of [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") are spoken in [Xuancheng](/wiki/Xuancheng "Xuancheng") [prefecture-level city](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city"), though these are rapidly being replaced by Jianghuai Mandarin; dialects of [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese") are spoken in a few counties in the southwest bordering Jiangxi province;[[38]](#cite_note-47) and the [Huizhou dialects](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese") are spoken in about ten counties in the far south, a small but highly diverse and unique group of Chinese dialects.

[Huangmeixi](/wiki/Huangmeixi "Huangmeixi"), which originated in the environs of [Anqing](/wiki/Anqing "Anqing") in southwestern Anhui, is a form of traditional [Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera") popular across China. [Huiju](/wiki/Huiju "Huiju"), a form of traditional opera originating in the [Huizhou](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese")-speaking areas of southern Anhui, is one of the major precursors of [Beijing Opera](/wiki/Beijing_Opera "Beijing Opera"); in the 1950s, Huiju (which had disappeared) was revived. [Luju](/wiki/Lu_opera "Lu opera") is a type of traditional opera found across central Anhui, from east to west.

[Anhui cuisine](/wiki/Anhui_cuisine "Anhui cuisine") is one of the eight great traditions of [Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine"). Combining elements of cooking from northern Anhui, south-central Anhui, and the Huizhou-speaking areas of southern Anhui, Anhui cuisine is known for its use of wild game and herbs, both land and sea, and comparatively simple methods of preparation.

Anhui has a high concentration of traditional products related to calligraphy: Xuanzhou (today [Xuancheng](/wiki/Xuancheng "Xuancheng")) and Huizhou (today [Huangshan City](/wiki/Huangshan_City "Huangshan City")) are revered for producing [Xuan Paper](/wiki/Xuan_Paper "Xuan Paper") and Hui [Ink](/wiki/Inkstick "Inkstick") respectively, which are traditionally considered the best types of paper and ink for [Chinese calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy"). [She County](/wiki/She_County,_Anhui "She County, Anhui") is famous for the [She Inkstone](/w/index.php?title=She_Inkstone&action=edit&redlink=1 "She Inkstone (page does not exist)"), one of the most preferred types of [inkstones](/wiki/Inkstone "Inkstone") (a required tool in traditional calligraphy).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
